description: It's a Long Way to the Top is bagpipes meeting electric guitar. The Jack is a blues song about venereal disease. Australia's finest export.
Bon Scott's last ride. The title track is perfect rock. Touch Too Much and Shot Down in Flames hit just as hard.
The mourning album that became the party album. Hells Bells opens with a funeral toll. You Shook Me All Night Long closes the argument.
The title track is eight minutes of Angus losing his mind. Whole Lotta Rosie is the anthem. Bad Boy Boogie doesn't quit.
My Baby Just Cares for Me became a hit 30 years later. Plain Gold Ring shows classical training meeting jazz instinct. Arrived fully formed.
Sinnerman is ten minutes of relentless piano and percussion. Strange Fruit from a Black woman's mouth hits different. A masterpiece.

album_title: I Put a Spell on You
description: Feeling Good became the standard. Ne me quitte pas in Nina's voice is devastating. The title track is pure command.
Four Women is a history of Black womanhood in four verses. Lilac Wine is heartbreak. The title track soars.
Reinvents a Randy Newman song as protest. Everything Must Change is Nina at her most reflective. A late career gem.
That tritone. The Devil's interval. Tony Iommi played it and invented a genre on a rainy day in Birmingham.
War Pigs, Iron Man, Fairies Wear Boots. Three all-time riffs on one album. Paranoid was written in five minutes as filler.
Sweet Leaf is an ode to weed. Children of the Grave is proto-thrash. Into the Void is the heaviest thing recorded in 1971.
Supernaut is the riff. Changes is the ballad nobody expected. Snowblind is about cocaine and doesn't hide it. Peak excess.
Dio joins. Neon Knights opens like a second debut. The title track is ten minutes of Dio at his most mythic.
No Woman, No Cry. Lively Up Yourself. First album with the new Wailers lineup. Revolution as a love letter.
Jamming, Three Little Birds, One Love — all one album. Natural Mystic opens like a prayer. Time called it album of the century.
The gentle one. Is This Love, Sun Is Shining, Easy Skanking. Love songs from a revolutionary on a day off.
Zimbabwe became an actual anthem for an actual revolution. Africa Unite, Wake Up and Live. The most political Marley.
Redemption Song is acoustic guitar and Bob's voice. Nothing else. Could You Be Loved is disco-reggae. His final studio album.
Southern Man lit a fuse. Only Love Can Break Your Heart is Neil at his gentlest. The title track is a fever dream.
Heart of Gold went number one and Neil spent decades running from it. Old Man and Needle and the Damage Done are devastating.
Recorded drunk, mourning two friends lost to heroin. The darkest album of the 70s. Raw and intentionally ugly.
Revolution Blues imagines the Manson Family. Ambulance Blues is eight minutes of scorched earth. Neil at his most bleak.
Acoustic side, electric side. Powderfinger might be his greatest song. My My, Hey Hey predicted punk.
Your Song. That's it. That's the entry point. Border Song and The King Must Die prove the range was there from day one.
Tiny Dancer took 30 years to become the anthem it deserved to be. Levon is a family epic in four minutes.
Double album, zero filler. Bennie and the Jets, Candle in the Wind, Saturday Night's Alright. The Taupin-John partnership at its peak.

album_title: Captain Fantastic and the Brown Dirt Cowboy
description: First album to debut at number one. Someone Saved My Life Tonight is autobiography as epic. The whole thing plays like a novel.
The comeback. I'm Still Standing is defiance as pop. I Guess That's Why They Call It the Blues brought Stevie Wonder on harmonica.
Wuthering Heights — written at 18, released at 19. The Man With the Child in His Eyes recorded at 16. Arrived from another planet.
Suspended in Gaffa is a fever. The title track is unhinged percussion and accents. Her label hated it. She was right.
Running Up That Hill on side one. The Ninth Wave suite on side two — a woman drowning, dreaming, surviving. Perfect front to back.
Twelve years of silence, then this. Pi is literally about the number. A Coral Room is about loss. An album that breathes.
Seven songs, each one long and patient. Snowflake floats. Lake Tahoe haunts. Stephen Fry counts to fifty. Winter as art.
Where Is My Mind? became the sound of a generation's breakdown. Bone Machine and Gigantic hit like trucks. Steve Albini recorded it raw.
Debaser opens with a scream about Un Chien Andalou. Monkey Gone to Heaven is ecology as noise pop. Here Comes Your Man is the secret pop song.
Space and surf. Velouria is gorgeous. Dig for Fire grooves. The most melodic Pixies record — still weird, just sunnier.
Michael Stipe mumbles and it's beautiful. Radio Free Europe, Talk About the Passion. College rock's founding document.
It's the End of the World as We Know It is the song everyone knows the chorus to. The One I Love broke them into the mainstream.
Everybody Hurts, Man on the Moon, Nightswimming. Three songs that define melancholy. Drive opens like a whisper and the album never shouts.
Losing My Religion — a mandolin riff that became inescapable. Shiny Happy People is the outlier. Country Feedback is the masterpiece.
Recorded on the road, sounds like it. E-Bow the Letter with Patti Smith. Electrolite closes the book on one era.
Unfinished Sympathy is the greatest trip-hop song ever recorded. Daydreaming and Safe From Harm set the template. Bristol invented a genre.
Teardrop became the House theme. Angel is seven minutes of dread. Dissolved Girl soundtracked The Matrix. Dark, paranoid, essential.
Everything But the Girl's Tracey Thorn sings the title track. Karmacoma creeps. Sly is a slow burn. The band finding its shadows.
Paradise Circus is a torch song from the abyss. Splitting the Atom is skeletal. Eight years in the making, sounds like controlled tension.
Solitude Is Bliss is the mission statement. Alter Ego swirls. Expectation builds for seven minutes then detonates. Psych rock from a bedroom in Perth.
Feels Like We Only Go Backwards is a pop song disguised as a spiral. Elephant stomps. Apocalypse Dreams melts and reforms. The leap.
Let It Happen is eight minutes of surrendering to change. The Less I Know the Better is the bassline everyone stole. New Person, Same Old Mistakes closed a chapter.
Posthumous Forgiveness is Kevin Parker talking to his dead father. Breathe Deeper grooves. On Track is quiet devastation disguised as synth pop.
Best Rapper Alive wasn't a boast, it was a fact. Fireman and Fly In don't let up. The moment Wayne went from hot to untouchable.
A Milli is a loop and a voice and nothing else. Lollipop went number one. Mr. Carter and Dr. Carter are Wayne at his sharpest. Went nuclear.
6 Foot 7 Foot is Wayne at maximum Weezy. She Will grooves. Nightmares of the Bottom is the deep cut. The album everyone was gonna hate but couldn't.
Don't Cry opens with X. Mona Lisa with Kendrick is a movie. Dedicate and Uproar prove six years away didn't dull anything.
Umbrella changed her entire trajectory. Don't Stop the Music and Shut Up and Drive kept the energy. The reinvention album.
Only Girl, What's My Name, S&M, Man Down, California King Bed. Five completely different singles and they all worked.
Work is a dancehall earworm. Kiss It Better is rock Rihanna. Needed Me is cold. The album she always wanted to make.
In Da Club, Many Men, 21 Questions. Three songs that defined 2003. Patiently Waiting with Em is the bonus. Bulletproof debut.
Candy Shop and Outta Control ran the radio. Piggy Bank was the diss track everyone talked about. Second round, still swinging.
I Used to Love H.E.R. is hip hop as a woman you're losing. The title track samples Ahmad Jamal. Chicago's Illmatic.
The Light is the greatest hip hop love song. The Questions and Dooinit hit different. J Dilla production throughout. Flawless.
Kanye produced the whole thing. The Corner, GO!, Faithful. Every track belongs. Short, no filler, all soul.
Kick Push is a skateboard anthem that's really about persistence. Daydreamin' and The Instrumental are Lupe at his densest. A debut with no wasted bars.
Superstar crossed over. Paris Tokyo grooves. The Coolest and Hip Hop Saved My Life are concept tracks that actually land.
Mural opens with seven minutes of unbroken bars. Dots & Lines and Prisoner 1 & 2 are layered deep. The comeback album that was fire doe.
Icy started everything. That's My Hood is a mission statement. The blueprint for trap before anyone called it that.
Lemonade and Heavy were radio Gucci. Spotlight and Wasted were club Gucci. The album that proved trap could go platinum.
First album after prison. Guwop Home is triumphant. Pop Music is Gucci being self-aware. A whole new Gucci, same ice.
Clint Eastwood is hip hop through a cartoon filter. Tomorrow Comes Today and 5/4 are Britpop's ghost. A debut that shouldn't work but does.
Feel Good Inc. is the bassline and De La Soul. DARE is the earworm. Every Planet We Reach Is Dead is the heartbreak. Dark, playful, huge.
On Melancholy Hill is pure beauty. Empire Ants builds from whisper to anthem. Stylo with Bobby Womack and Mos Def is a car chase. The ambitious one.
Pump It Up was the club hit. The rest of the album is darker, realer. Focus and Survivor show what Budden does when the cameras leave.
Broke and Immortal are peak Budden introspection. The production is moody throughout. The album for when you need a good rant.
Hard in da Paint is exactly what it sounds like. No Hands ran every party. O Let's Do It started a movement. Pure energy, zero apologies.
3005 is the hit. The Library and Sweatpants are the deep cuts. A concept album about the internet that actually works. Feel dirty for liking it.
Redbone is a Funkadelic dream. Me and Your Mama opens with a scream. Terrified is Prince-level falsetto. The pivot nobody expected.
Bank Account is the hook. No Heart is the attitude. Atlanta trap stripped to the bone.
Metro Boomin and Offset. Ghostface Killers. Halloween release, horror movie beats. A collab tape that plays like a real album.
Morgan Freeman narrates. Runnin is the single. Metro's production is cinematic. The sequel that justified itself.
The ICE arrest album. Redrum is the hit. Personal, political, still hits hard.
Chasing Pavements and a voice that shouldn't exist in a teenager. The debut that got Grammy voters shaking.
Rolling in the Deep. Someone Like You. Biggest album of the decade, full stop.
Hello broke YouTube. When We Were Young is the sleeper. Three years of silence and she came back bigger.
Divorce album. Easy On Me is the single but Oh My God is the energy. Grown woman heartbreak.
Archie, Marry Me is indie pop perfection. Adult Diversion jangles. A debut that sounds like summer ending.
Dreams Tonite is the standout. In Undertow shimmers. Shoegaze meets power pop, tighter than the debut.
Pharmacist opens like a wall of noise. Belladonna of Sadness is the prettiest thing they've done. Five years worth the wait.

album_title: Whatever People Say I Am, That's What I'm Not
description: I Bet You Look Good on the Dancefloor. Fastest-selling UK debut ever. Alex Turner was 19.
Do I Wanna Know? R U Mine? Stoner desert rock with a hip-hop swagger. The riff album.
Fluorescent Adolescent. Brianstorm. Louder, faster, angrier. Sheffield at full volume.
Lounge rock on the moon. Four Out of Five. No guitars, no problem. Turner becomes a crooner.
Dark Entries. Stigmata Martyr. Post-punk as seance. The first goth album.
Kick in the Eye. The Passion of Lovers. Danceable darkness.
bmbmbm is a panic attack set to music. Near DT, MI is a freight train. London's most unhinged debut.
John L opens with a marching band from hell. Chondromalacia Patella is prog-jazz chaos. Tighter but no less insane.
Sugar/Tzu. Welcome to Hell. A rock opera about war, food, and madness. Their best.
More Than a Feeling. Peace of Mind. Foreplay/Long Time. Tom Scholz engineered perfection in a basement. 17 million sold.
The title track carries it. A Man I'll Never Be shows range. Couldn't top the debut but few could.
Not Strong Enough. Emily I'm Sorry. Cool About It. Three artists at their peak making something none could alone.
Peggy Sue. Everyday. I'm Gonna Love You Too. Rock and roll's first auteur. The glasses. The hiccup.
The Crickets. That'll Be the Day and Oh, Boy! Two songs that built the template for guitar pop.
The accessible one. I'm Gonna Booglarize You Baby. Blues-rock that almost makes sense. His most listenable album.
Door is the opener. So Hot You're Hurting My Feelings is the sleeper hit. Art pop with actual hooks.

album_title: Desire, I Want to Turn Into You
description: Bunny Is a Rider. Welcome to My Island. Flamenco guitar meets hyperpop. Unclassifiable and perfect.

album_title: The Rise and Fall of a Midwest Princess
description: Good Luck, Babe! Red Wine Supernova. Hot to Go! Queer pop anthems from a drag-inspired stage show. Zero to everywhere.
Beginnings. Does Anybody Really Know What Time It Is? Double album debut. Jazz-rock with horns before anyone else.
25 or 6 to 4 is the riff. Make Me Smile. Another double album, another classic. Horns blazing.
Saturday in the Park. Dialogue. Their first number one album. The hit machine starts.
Hard Habit to Break. You're the Inspiration. Peter Cetera era. Ballads that ruled the radio.
Holiday in Cambodia. California Uber Alles. Kill the Poor. The most quotable punk album ever made.
Halloween. Moon Over Marin. Harder, faster, more political. Jello Biafra's satire sharpened.
Sultans of Swing. Knopfler's fingerpicking debut. No pick, no distortion, just tone.
Money for Nothing. Walk of Life. First major CD-era album. Sold 30 million. The MTV song that bit the hand.
Romeo and Juliet is the love song. Tunnel of Love is the epic. Skateaway grooves. Peak storytelling.
Telegraph Road is 14 minutes of Knopfler narrative. Private Investigations is film noir as a single.
Say So went TikTok viral. Juicy. Boss Bitch. The album that turned a meme rapper into a pop star.
Kiss Me More with SZA. Need to Know. Woman. Space-themed pop-rap perfection.
Paint the Town Red flipped a sample and went number one. Demons. Agora Hills. Dark Doja.
The title track invented psychedelic folk-pop. Season of the Witch is the deep cut everyone knows.
That drone riff. Atlantis is spoken word hippie mythology. Jimmy Page plays guitar on the title track.
Quite rightly. The title track was the hit. Jennifer Juniper was written about Jenny Boyd. Laurel Canyon's Scottish visitor.
The A Team. Lego House. A busker's debut that sold 10 million. Loop pedal magic.
Thinking Out Loud. Sing with Pharrell. Don't with Rick Rubin. The album that made him inescapable.
Shape of You. Castle on the Hill. Galway Girl. Biggest tour of all time followed. Math keeps working.
The title track is the synth riff. Love Is a Stranger. Annie Lennox's androgynous power on full display.
Would I Lie to You? There Must Be an Angel. Sisters Are Doin' It with Aretha. Pop-soul peak.
Classy 101. Normal. Reggaeton at its most melodic. Medellín's party starter.
Fumeteo. Collaboration heavy. The album that connected the Colombian scene.
Boys in the Better Land. Big. Dublin poetry over post-punk. A debut that sounds lived-in.
The title track loops like a mantra. I Don't Belong is the gut punch. Darker, slower, deeper.
Starburster. The genre pivot. Shoegaze, electronic, still unmistakably them. Mercury Prize nominated.
Take Me Out. The Dark of the Matinée. Indie disco born in Glasgow. That riff change in Take Me Out is the moment.

album_title: You Could Have It So Much Better
description: Do You Want To. The Fallen. Walk Away. Louder, sharper, faster. The difficult second album that wasn't.
Killing Me Softly. Ready or Not. Fu-Gee-La. 22 million sold. Lauryn Hill dominates. One of the greatest rap albums ever.
Cirice. From the Pinnacle to the Pit. Heavy metal as Broadway show. Grammy winner.
Rats. Dance Macabre. Arena rock with Satanic window dressing. The crossover album.
Twenties. Kaisarion. Stadium metal. Tobias Forge keeps reinventing the gimmick.
Flying Whales. The song every metal fan knows. French progressive death metal about environmentalism. Heavy beyond measure.
Oroborus. Toxic Garbage Island. Tighter, faster, angrier. The evolution continues.
Stranded. Silvera. Personal grief meets crushing riffs. Their most emotional album.
Pull Up to the Bumper. Walking in the Rain. Demolition Man. Sly & Robbie production. Reggae-new wave fusion. An icon's defining moment.
Private Life. The title track is industrial reggae. Love Is the Drug reimagined. The Island Records reinvention.
One song, seven versions. Trevor Horn production excess. The title track is her signature.
Oblivion. Genesis. Recorded in two weeks on GarageBand in a dark room. Lo-fi art pop that launched a career.
Kill V. Maim. Flesh Without Blood. Pop pivot. Polished but still alien.
My Name Is Dark. 4ÆM. Climate doom as synth-pop. Her darkest and most focused.
Take Me to Church. Cherry Wine. From Eden. Irish folk-blues that went global on one song's power.
Too Sweet. Francesca. Dante's Inferno as folk-rock. His most ambitious and it worked.
Think U The Shit (Fart). Gimmie a Light. Bronx drill minimalism. The debut that the memes demanded.
WHATS POPPIN. Tyler Herro. Louisville's golden boy arrives. Charm over bars.

album_title: Come Home the Kids Miss You
description: First Class. Nail Tech. Fergie sample flipped into a number one. The commercial album.
Me and Bobby McGee. Mercedes Benz. Cry Baby. Released posthumously. The rawness that couldn't be contained.

album_title: I Got Dem Ol' Kozmic Blues Again Mama!
description: Try (Just a Little Bit Harder). Kozmic Blues. Solo Janis with horns. San Francisco's loudest voice.
In Heaven. Rugged Country. Grief turned into shimmering indie pop. Michelle Zauner's debut after loss.
Be Sweet. Paprika opens in Technicolor. Joy as a radical act after two albums of mourning.
The title track is a standard. Romantic balladry from Mexico's greatest. Mariachi meets pop.
Querida. The performance is as important as the recording. Live energy captured.
TQG with Shakira. Mientras Me Curo del Cora. Tomorrow will be beautiful. Latin pop's biggest album of the year.
Bichota. Location. El Makinón. The album that made her La Bichota worldwide.
Mistakes Like Fractures. Trapped in the Grasp of a Memory. Metalcore that doesn't apologize.

album_title: You Won't Go Before You're Supposed To
description: Suffocate. Blinding Faith. The heaviest album of 2024. Hardcore for people who needed it.
Suzanne. So Long, Marianne. Sisters of Mercy. A poet picks up a guitar and changes everything.
Hallelujah. The label rejected it. Then everyone covered it. Then it became the most performed song in the world.
Everybody Knows. Tower of Song. First, We Take Manhattan. Synths and a deeper voice. The reinvention.
The title track. Released three weeks before he died. Hineni, hineni — I'm ready, my Lord.

album_title: Come Over When You're Sober, Pt. 1
description: Awful Things. Benz Truck. Emo trap before it had a name. Gone three months after release.

album_title: Come Over When You're Sober, Pt. 2
description: Runaway. Life Is Beautiful. Posthumous but mostly finished. The sadness hits different knowing.
Chaeri. You Lose! Synth-pop as virtual reality. A simulation you don't want to leave.
Killing Time. Image. Bigger, weirder, more ambitious. One of the best albums of 2024.
This Love. She Will Be Loved. Harder to Breathe. Before the pop pivot. An actual band making actual funk-rock.
Payphone. One More Night. Max Martin joins. The transformation into a pop machine is complete.
Blood and Thunder. Iron Tusk. Moby Dick as a sludge-metal concept album. The white whale of heavy music.
Oblivion. The Czar. Astral projection and Rasputin. Progressive metal's masterpiece of the 2000s.
Colony of Birchmen. Crystal Skull. The bridge between sludge and prog. Relentless.
Savage remix with Beyoncé. Body. Cry Baby with DaBaby. Hot Girl Meg's victory lap after a nightmare year.
Dollhouse. Pity Party. Sippy Cup. Childhood horror as pop concept. Every song is a scene.
DEATH. VOID. The rebirth concept. Stranger, darker. She directed the whole visual album.
Time to Pretend. Kids. Electric Feel. Blog-era psychedelic pop. Three perfect singles and a generation's soundtrack.
The title track went TikTok viral years later. When You Die. Me and Michael. The comeback nobody saw coming.
Flash Delirium. Siberian Breaks is 12 minutes of prog. No singles. The anti-commercial move. Respect.
The Rain. Sock It 2 Me. Timbaland beats that didn't sound like anything else. Hip hop's future arrived.
Get Ur Freak On. One Minute Man. That tabla beat changed everything. The song that still sounds futuristic.
Work It. Is it worth it? Let me work it. Put my thing down flip it and reverse it. Enough said.
Little Lion Man. The Cave. Banjo folk-rock that filled arenas overnight. Launched a thousand imitators.
I Will Wait. Below My Feet. More banjo. Grammy for Album of the Year. The formula works.
White Elephant. Hand of God. Warren Ellis collaboration. Made in lockdown. Death, loss, beauty. Late-period masterwork.
Don't Speak. Just a Girl. Spiderweb. Ska-punk goes platinum. Gwen Stefani becomes a star.
Hella Good. Hey Baby. Underneath It All. Dancehall and new wave. The genre-bending reinvention.
Drivers License. Good 4 U. Deja Vu. A breakup album from a teenager that sold 15 million. Brutal.
Vampire. Bad Idea Right? Get Him Back! Pop-punk sophomore album. Not a fluke.
Story of My Life. Best Song Ever. The rock-leaning one. Five boys growing up on record.
Night Changes. Steal My Girl. Fireproof. Maturity. The album that hinted at five solo careers.
Apologize with Timbaland. Stop and Stare. Ryan Tedder's songwriting on display. Pop-rock for the late 2000s.
Counting Stars. If I Lose Myself. The streaming era smash. That bass line is inescapable.

album_title: A Fever You Can't Sweat Out
description: I Write Sins Not Tragedies. Lying Is the Most Fun. Emo baroque pop. The title alone tells you everything.
Victorious. Emperor's New Clothes. Brendon Urie solo vehicle by now. Theatrical maximalism.
Gloria reimagined. Jesus died for somebody's sins but not mine. Punk's poet laureate. Mapplethorpe took the cover.
People Have the Power. The comeback after a decade of silence. Fred Sonic Smith on guitar.
Ella Baila Sola. La People. Corridos tumbados went global. Mexico's new voice for a new generation.
Lady Gaga. Hollywood. The follow-up that proved Génesis wasn't a fluke. Bigger stages.
Number One with Kanye. Can I Have It Like That with Gwen. Neptune beats for a solo debut.
Happy soundtracked the world for a year. Marilyn Monroe and Come Get It Bae are the deep cuts.
In the Air Tonight. That drum fill. I Missed Again. Genesis drummer goes solo and outsells the band.
Sussudio. One More Night. Don't Lose My Number. Five Grammys. The definition of 80s pop-rock dominance.
Another Day in Paradise. Something Happened on the Way to Heaven. The serious one. Still sold millions.
Boy's a Liar Pt. 2 with Ice Spice. Capable of Love. Two-minute songs. Drum'n'bass nostalgia from bedroom pop.
The title track. Steve Albini production. Raw, loud, confrontational. Polly Jean arrived screaming.
Down by the Water. C'mon Billy. Blues-gothic reinvention. The one that crossed over.

album_title: Stories From the City, Stories From the Sea
description: Good Fortune. A Place Called Home. This Is Love. Mercury Prize winner. Her most accessible.
The Words That Maketh Murder. On Battleship Hill. War album. Mercury Prize again. England's conscience.
Glory Box. Sour Times. Wandering Star. Trip-hop invented. Beth Gibbons' voice over vinyl crackle. Perfect.
All Mine. Over. Cowboys. Darker, more claustrophobic. Self-titled and self-assured.
Machine Gun. The Rip. Silence for 11 years then this. Industrial, krautrock, unrecognizable. Terrifying.
White Iverson and Congratulations. Genre-proof. A frat party with feelings.
Rockstar with 21 Savage. Psycho. Better Now. He owned the summer.
Circles is the pivot. Sunflower with Swae Lee is Spider-Verse perfection.
A Whiter Shade of Pale. That Bach-inspired organ. Conquistador. Psychedelic baroque rock. The debut that echoed forever.
The title track is symphonic rock before prog existed. Wreck of the Hesperus. Their most ambitious.
Todo de Ti is the summer anthem. Aquel Nap ZzZz. Puerto Rican R&B with reggaeton pulse.
Full salsa pivot. De Carolina. Respect for the roots. Latin pop's smoothest operator goes traditional.
Virginia Plain. Re-Make/Re-Model. Brian Eno's synths meet Bryan Ferry's croon. Art rock arrives in a tuxedo.
In Every Dream Home a Heartache. Editions of You. Eno's last album with them. The weird peak.
More Than This. The title track. Silk and synths. The smoothest album ever made. Ferry in his final form.
Nonsense. Skinny Dipping. The transition from Disney kid to pop artist. The outro ad-libs started here.
Espresso. Please Please Please. Taste. The summer album. Petite frame, massive hooks.
Spellbound. Arabian Knights. Night Shift. Gothic post-punk's defining album.
Hong Kong Garden. Jigsaw Feeling. The debut. Punk evolving into something stranger.
Slowdive. Cascade. Lush, psychedelic, gorgeous. The romantic side of goth.
Cowboys. Serial Killer. Y2K pop maximalism with a hyperpop edge. No filter, no shame.
Narrator. Pamphlets. Seven minutes of controlled post-punk panic. Brighton's most chaotic export.
School. Dreamer. Bloody Well Right. Art-prog that sold. The Wurlitzer enters the chat.
The Logical Song. Take the Long Way Home. Goodbye Stranger. 20 million sold. Pop-prog perfection.
Give a Little Bit. Fool's Overture. The gentle one between the masterpieces.
Don't You Worry Child. Save the World. Greyhound. The reunion album. EDM's holy trinity returns.
Free Mind. Love Me JeJe. Nigerian Afrobeats meets R&B soul. The debut that justified every feature verse.
Mr. Brightside. Somebody Told Me. All These Things That I've Done. Perfect debut. Every track a single.
When You Were Young is the anthem. Read My Mind is the grower. Springsteen in the desert.
Human. Spaceman. Synth-pop pivot. Are we human or are we dancer.
Caution opens like a movie. Blowback is the buried gem. The comeback nobody expected.

album_title: If You Can Believe Your Eyes and Ears
description: California Dreamin'. Monday Monday. Four-part harmonies that defined Laurel Canyon. The dream starts here.
Self Esteem. Come Out and Play. Gotta Get Away. 11 million on Epitaph. Indie label punk gone nuclear.
Pretty Fly for a White Guy. The Kids Aren't Alright. Why Don't You Get a Job? Pop-punk for the masses.
I Wanna Be Your Dog. No Fun. 1969. Iggy Pop and three chords. Punk five years early.
Down on the Street. TV Eye. Loose. Raw Power before Raw Power. The heaviest album of 1970.
Bitter Sweet Symphony. The Drugs Don't Work. Lucky Man. Britpop's last great album before it all ended.
History. On Your Own. Psychedelic shoegaze before they found the strings. The underrated one.
The Boys Are Back in Town. Jailbreak. Emerald. Twin guitar harmonies. Phil Lynott's Dublin poetry meets hard rock.
Waiting for an Alibi. Do Anything You Want To. Róisín Dubh. Gary Moore on guitar. The peak.
Dancing in the Moonlight. Opium Trail. Killer on the Loose. Lynott as rock's most charismatic frontman.
Oye Como Va before Santana made it famous. Hong Kong Mambo. El Rey del Timbal on full display. The one.
Para los rumberos. Latin jazz perfection. The timbales are everything.
Waterfalls. Creep. Red Light Special. 23 million sold. The best-selling album by an American girl group.
No Scrubs. Unpretty. Dear Lie. Futuristic R&B. Dallas Austin and Babyface production.
Mystery. Blackout. Holiday. Hardcore meets summer energy. The album that broke hardcore into the mainstream.
Real Thing. Generator. Bomb. Hardcore punk with hooks. The bridge between underground and daylight.
Stressed Out. Ride. Tear in My Heart. Every track charted. Diamond album. Tyler Joseph's anxiety as arena pop.
Holding on to You. Car Radio. Ode to Sleep. The indie album before the explosion. Already great.
Jumpsuit. Chlorine. My Blood. Concept album. Darker, more ambitious. Dema mythology.
Y.M.C.A. The song. In the Navy. Every sports event, every wedding. Camp as cultural institution.
Chaise Longue. Wet Dream. Ur Mum. Deadpan Isle of Wight indie with hooks for days. Mercury-nominated debut.
Maps. Date With the Night. Pin. Karen O's feral energy captured on tape. Art-punk perfection.
Zero. Heads Will Roll. Synth-dance pivot. The glam reinvention. Still wild.
Poppin. Dëja vu. Autonomous trap. The beats swallow everything. Rage music for the algorithm era.
Breathe. Drake feature. Mainstream arrival. Same energy, bigger stages.
The London. Hot. Surf. Bad Bad Bad. Atlanta trap elevated to pop art.
Acoustic guitars and singing. Thugger goes left. Droppin Jewels with Travis Scott.
Released while incarcerated. Unfinished but defiant.
Something in the Orange. Heading South. Open the Gate. Triple album. Country folk from a Navy vet's heart.
Tourniquet. I Remember Everything with Kacey Musgraves. Oklahoma. The self-titled statement.
Pink Skies. 28. Bigger stages, same sincerity. Country's most authentic voice going.
Clarity with Foxes. Spectrum. EDM pop crossover. Grammy winner. The song that brought electronic to pop radio.
Beautiful Now. I Want You to Know with Selena Gomez. Pop-EDM's polished peak.
Dear Mama. So Many Tears. Recorded while awaiting prison. His most vulnerable and his best.
California Love. How Do U Want It. Double album, 27 tracks, released months before his death. Ambition as legacy.
Brenda's Got a Baby. Trapped. A 20-year-old from Marin City already writing about poverty and police violence.
Keep Ya Head Up. I Get Around. The range — tenderness and aggression on the same album.
If Your Girl Only Knew. One in a Million. Timbaland and Missy Elliott production. The template for 2000s R&B.
Try Again. More Than a Woman. Rock the Boat. Released two months before the plane crash. Her most complete work.

album_title: Age Ain't Nothing but a Number
description: Back & Forth. The title track. R. Kelly produced it. She was 15. The voice was already fully formed.
Dancing Queen. Money Money Money. Fernando. Sweden's greatest cultural export at its peak. Perfect pop architecture.
The title track won Eurovision and changed everything. Honey Honey. Ring Ring. Four people becoming one machine.
The Winner Takes It All is a real breakup song written about a real breakup. Agnetha and Björn had just divorced. It shows.
I Still Have Faith in You. Don't Shut Me Down. 40 years later. Digital avatars, but the songwriting is real.
Walk This Way. Sweet Emotion. Toys in the Attic. The album that made them legends before the drugs almost erased it.
Love in an Elevator. Janie's Got a Gun. F.I.N.E. The clean comeback hit harder than the original.
Back in the Saddle. Last Child. Combination. Their darkest and their heaviest. The critics ignored it. Wrong.
Dude Looks Like a Lady. Angel. Rag Doll. The MTV era arrival. Slick but they earned it.
The title track is the greatest soul single ever recorded. L-O-V-E. Look What You Done for Me. Willie Mitchell production. Perfection.

album_title: I'm Still in Love With You
description: I'm Still in Love With You. Simply Beautiful. Call Me. Two masterpieces in one year. He was 26.
Call Me. Here I Am. You Ought to Be with Me. The third classic album in a row. Nobody was doing it like this.
Belle. Georgia Boy. The gospel turn begins. He produced it himself. Different from everything before it, still essential.
The one that announced him. Parisienne Walkways is the greatest slow blues guitar solo committed to tape.
Hard rock Gary before the blues took over. Wishing Well rips.
Over the Hills and Far Away. Celtic melodies over a rock engine. Ireland in every note.
The pivot. Dropped the shred, picked up the feeling. Title track is six minutes of pure ache.
Late-night blues. Story of the Blues and the Albert King duet. A glass of whiskey in album form.
The one. Remember is the prettiest hard bop melody ever written. Rudy Van Gelder behind the glass. Flawless.
Freddie Hubbard on trumpet, Art Blakey on drums. The Take Off is pure jet fuel.
Workout is exactly what it sounds like. Mobley stretching, Wynton Kelly comping, Philly Joe driving.
The title says it all. Three Coins in the Fountain swings so hard it hurts.
Gin and Juice. Who Am I. The debut that defined G-funk. Dr. Dre production at its smoothest, Snoop gliding over every beat.
Post-Dre, post-Death Row tension. Snoop Upside Ya Head still bangs. Darker, hungrier.
Drop It Like It's Hot with Pharrell. Signs with Geffen, reinvents himself again. The Neptunes connection was a cheat code.
Lay Low with Nate Dogg. Master P era Snoop still finding gems. Wrong Idea goes hard.
The Mercury debut with Clifford Brown on trumpet. Lullaby of Birdland is the definitive version. Period.
Lush Life done the way Billy Strayhorn heard it in his head. Intimate, devastating, perfect.
Two volumes of Gershwin. The voice is an instrument that makes the orchestra jealous.
With the Basie band. Smoke Gets in Your Eyes swings like nothing else. The voice meets the machine.
Sober. Prison Sex. The debut that crawled out of the LA underground dripping with anger and precision.
Stinkfist opens and you know the rules changed. Eulogy, H., Pushit. Progressive metal before anyone called it that.
Schism. The Grudge. Fibonacci sequences in the time signatures. The album that proved prog-metal could be spiritual.
Vicarious. Jambi. Wings for Marie is ten minutes about his mother that will break you.
Thirteen years of waiting. Pneuma. Descending. Eighty minutes of patience rewarded.
The Wilhelm Scream. Limit to Your Love. A debut that invented a sound — post-dubstep soul, voice cracking through sub-bass. Nothing sounded like this before.
Mercury Prize winner. Retrograde is the centrepiece — haunted, massive, gorgeous. Life Round Here with Chance the Rapper.
Sprawling, 17 tracks, Rick Rubin and Bon Iver in the room. Radio Silence and I Need a Forest Fire. His most vulnerable.
Mile High with Travis Scott and Metro Boomin. Barefoot in the Park with Rosalia. The loner opened the door.
Angels. The song that made the solo career. Old Before I Die kicks it off, but Angels closes the deal. Forever.
Millennium. Strong. No Regrets with Neil Tennant and Neil Hannon. The swagger album.
Rock DJ. Better Man. Let Love Be Your Energy. Peak Robbie — every single was a hit, every video was an event.
Feel. The ballad that proved he could do tender without irony. Something Beautiful and Sexed Up round it out.
Such a Shame. The title track. Synth pop done right — hooky, melancholic, massive in Europe.
Life's What You Make It. The bridge album — still pop, but the cracks of something deeper showing through.
The left turn that invented post-rock. Recorded in the dark with 20+ session musicians. EMI hated it. History loved it.
Ascension Day. After the Flood. The final album. Quieter than Spirit of Eden, somehow even more devastating. Mark Hollis walked away after this.
The debut. A Warning sets the tone — dub basslines, trip-hop atmospherics, DC in the global mix.
Lebanese Blonde. Indra. Le Monde. The perfect downtempo record. Every track belongs on a mixtape for someone you love.
Heaven's Gonna Burn Your Eyes. Deeper, darker, more political. The Outernationalist is the mission statement.
Portuguese for longing. Bossa nova front to back. Decollage is silk.
Vinushka is 9 minutes of progressive metal perfection. The album where they stopped being a visual kei band and became something untouchable.
Latin for while I breathe, I hope. Their heaviest, most complex work. Different Sense is the entry point. Diabolos is the deep end.
The Western crossover attempt. Conceived Sorrow. Agitated Screams of Maggots. Raw, furious, uncompromising.
Dead Tree. Saku. The transition album — still traces of visual kei beauty inside the growing heaviness.
48 Crash. Glycerine Queen. The debut that put a woman with a bass guitar on Top of the Pops and dared anyone to say something.
The Wild One. Devil Gate Drive. Harder, louder, meaner. Chinn and Chapman writing hits, Suzi delivering them like a punch.
The title says it all. I Bit Off More Than I Could Chew is self-aware rock and roll. She knew exactly what she was doing.
Stumblin' In with Chris Norman — the soft side nobody expected. If You Can't Give Me Love crossed her over.
Caught by the Fuzz. Alright. A debut made by teenagers that sounds like teenagers — fast, loud, grinning. Pure sugar rush.
Richard III opens like a freight train. Darker, heavier, more ambitious. Late in the Day is the hidden gut-punch.
Moving. Pumping on Your Stereo. The self-titled where they proved the debut wasn't a fluke. T-Rex glam meets Oxford indie.
St. Petersburg. Low C. The quiet one. Stripped back, reflective, beautiful. Nobody was expecting this from the Alright guys.

album_title: Save the Last Dance for Me
description: The title track. Ben E. King left but the magic stayed. I Count the Tears. This Magic Moment. Strings and heartbreak.
The definitive summer song. On Broadway. One Way Love. Every track sounds like salt air and sunburn.
Pull Me Under hit MTV and prog-metal had a mainstream moment. Another Day. Take the Time. The album that launched a thousand Guitar Center auditions.
6:00. The Mirror. Scarred is 11 minutes of technical fury. Darker than Images and Words, meaner, Kevin Moore's last stand.

album_title: Metropolis, Pt. 2: Scenes From a Memory
description: A concept album about past lives and murder. The Spirit Carries On. Home. Their magnum opus — every prog fan's desert island pick.
The Glass Prison. Misunderstood. Disc two is a 42-minute suite. Excessive by design, brilliant by execution.
The title track is 24 minutes. Panic Attack shreds. The Answer Lies Within is the ballad. The album that ends exactly where it begins.
Insomnia. Salva Mea. Don't Leave. The debut that fused house, trip-hop, and spoken word into something church-like. Maxi Jazz as the preacher.
God Is a DJ. Take the Long Way Home. Bring My Family Back. Bigger production, bigger stages, same soul.
We Come 1. Muhammad Ali. One Step Too Far with Dido. The political album — dance music with something to say.
Mass Destruction. The title track. Post-Iraq anger over breakbeats. Maxi Jazz at his most urgent.
The debut that launched a thousand think pieces. Hit Me Baby One More Time. Sometimes. Crazy. Max Martin built a machine and Britney was the engine.
Stronger. Lucky. The title track. Doubled down on everything and sold 20 million copies. The red jumpsuit on Mars.
Gimme More. Piece of Me. Made during the worst year of her life and it sounds like the future. Danja and Bloodshy & Avant built a masterpiece around the chaos.
Toxic. The hook, the strings, the Bollywood sample. Me Against the Music with Madonna. The album where she took creative control.
Genie in a Bottle. What a Girl Wants. I Turn to You. The debut that went 8x platinum. Pure teen pop perfection, voice already bigger than the genre.
Beautiful. Fighter. Dirrty. The reinvention that shocked everyone. She threw away the teen pop playbook and showed up with chaps and a voice that could level buildings.
Double album. Ain't No Other Man. Candyman. Jazz, soul, blues — she went retro and the voice finally had a home that fit.
Accelerate. Fall in Line with Demi Lovato. Ten years between albums and she came back angry. Kanye, Anderson .Paak, Ty Dolla $ign in the credits.
Purple Haze. Hey Joe. Foxy Lady. The Wind Cries Mary. The debut that rewrote the rules of what a guitar could do. Every note a revolution.
Little Wing. Spanish Castle Magic. Bold as Love. Eight months after the debut and somehow more beautiful. The ballad side of genius.
All Along the Watchtower. Voodoo Child (Slight Return). Crosstown Traffic. Double album, studio as instrument, the peak of psychedelic rock. Nothing came close.
Mack the Knife. Beyond the Sea. The album that turned a rock and roll kid into a standards singer overnight. Sinatra noticed.
Splish Splash. The debut. Teen idol energy, novelty hit magic. He was 22 and already restless for something bigger.

album_title: Niandra LaDes and Usually Just a T-Shirt
description: Recorded during the darkest period. My Smile Is a Rifle. Raw, broken, lo-fi beauty. The sound of a genius falling apart and still making art.

album_title: To Record Only Water for Ten Days
description: Going Inside. Someone's. The comeback album after getting clean. Electronic textures over acoustic bones. Tender, searching, alive.
Carvel. Regret. Omission. His most complete solo record — Chili Peppers polish with solo album freedom. Should have been huge.
The Past Recedes. Anne. Control. Acoustic, intimate, gorgeous. The one to start with if you've only heard him with RHCP.
Before the Beginning. Unreachable. A 50-minute journey through space and grief. His Dark Side of the Moon.
Mamma Mia. Gimme Gimme Gimme. Super Trouper. ABBA covers by Swedish teenagers that had no business being this fun. The whole album is a party.
Upside Down. Halfway Around the World. Their own material now, still bouncy, still sugar-coated, still works.
Only in My Dreams. Shake Your Love. Out of the Blue. She wrote every song at 16. The youngest artist to write, produce, and perform a #1 single.
Lost in Your Eyes. No More Rhyme. Electric Youth. The title track is pure optimism in a bottle. #1 album at 18.
Confident. Cool for the Summer. Stone Cold is the ballad that proved everything. The album where she stopped being a Disney artist and became a force.
Sorry Not Sorry. Tell Me You Love Me. Lonely. Vocally her best — every song is a showcase and she makes it look effortless.
Heart Attack. Neon Lights. Made in the USA. The commercial peak. Heart Attack alone is worth the price of admission.
So Yesterday. Come Clean. Why Not. Triple platinum. The soundtrack to 2003 if you were 13 and alive.
With Love. Stranger. The grown-up album that deserved more. Electropop before electropop was everywhere.
Beautiful Soul. She's No You. The title track was inescapable in 2004. He co-wrote Bleeding Love two years later. Underestimated.
Leavin'. It's Over. The R&B pivot that actually worked. Timbaland-adjacent production, real songwriting chops.
I Wanna Love You Forever. I Think I'm in Love With You. The debut. That voice — big, clean, undeniable. Pop done right.
With You. Sweetest Sin. The post-Newlyweds album that sold 4 million. With You is genuinely beautiful.
Candy. So Real. Walk Me Home. Fifteen years old, bubblegum pop, instant hit. The voice was always better than the material.
Extraordinary. All Good Things. The reinvention. Indie-folk Mandy, writing her own songs, Ryan Adams producing. The album that earned the respect.
Rebelde. Solo Quedate en Silencio. The debut from the Rebelde telenovela cast. Sold 15 million worldwide. Latin pop perfection.
Ser o Parecer. Dame. Celestial. The peak — stadium tours, global reach, six kids from Mexico City on top of the world.
Be-Bop Baby. Honeycomb. The debut at 17. TV star turned rock and roller. The girls screamed, the music was real.
Travelin' Man. Hello Mary Lou. The grown-up record. Travelin' Man is one of the most perfect pop singles ever cut.
Come Back to Me. Say OK. Let Go. The solo debut with more R&B edge than anyone expected from Gabriella Montez.
